Fall of Ahom Kingdom

# Fall of Ahom Kingdom

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Mar 19, 2021

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

Moamoria rebellion was an event that precipitated the decline of the [Ahom](https://www.quora.com/Why-did-the-Ahom-kingdom-fall/answer/Ratnakar-Sadasyula-%E0%B0%B0%E0%B0%A4%E0%B1%8D%E0%B0%A8%E0%B0%BE%E0%B0%95%E0%B0%B0%E0%B1%8D-%E0%B0%B8%E0%B0%A6%E0%B0%B8%E0%B1%8D%E0%B0%AF%E0%B1%81%E0%B0%B2?__filter__=5&__nsrc__=1&__sncid__=13245784626&__snid3__=18828190175) kingdom. One of the factors was the failure of the traditional Paik system to adapt to the rapidly changing economic and social aspirations. For the Ahom rulers, the Paik system was the foundation on which they based their administration, their kingdom, their society.

**What was the Paik System?**

Basically it was the system on which the entire Ahom kingdom was based, a medieval labor system of sorts. Paik was used to designate every able bodied male between age of 15 and 50, who was not a noble, a high priest,nor belonging to a high caste nor a slave either. He had to render service to the Ahom state, in return of which he got, 2 puras( 2.26 acres) of cultivable land which was not hereditary. The paiks had to offer services to the royals, that included defense. Till the start of the 19th century, the Ahom rulers had no standing army, and the Paiks usually made up the bulk of their army during war time. Apart from defense, they also had to offer services in construction of embankments, bridges, roads and production of defense equipment. It was pretty much an organized labor network. For more details you can check out here[Paik system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paik_system "en.wikipedia.org")

Anyway coming back to the main topic, like most other systems, the Paik system, began to flounder, thanks to the internal corruption by nobles, and it’s failure to handle a monetized economy that was coming into place soon. And the main factor, for most of the lower classes, tribesmen who served in the Paika, it soon became a kind of bonded labor.

The Vaishnava Sattaras on the other hand, began to appeal to the masses with their egalitarian ethos and to many tribesmen, lower classes, offered a release from the Paik system. The Mahapuruxiya Dharma established by Srimanta Sankaradeva, offered an escape for those working under the Paik system. The Sattaras became a safe refuge from the compulsory labor under the Paik system. Predictably the conflict between the Ahom rulers and the Sattaras began, with the former seeing it as a threat to their existing system. Sankaradeva himself had to flee to the Koch kingdom during reign of Suklemung to avoid the persecution. While Jayadhwaj Singha, adopted a more tolerant policy towards the Sattaras, and his succesors up to Sulikhpa followed it, once again Gadhadhar Singha, began to persecute the Sattaras.

Rudra Singha on the other hand, followed a divide and rule policy, encouraging Brahmin Sattaras, and pitting them against non Brahmin Sattaras. When the policy was not working, he directly began to encourage Shaktism, an ideology to which the Sattaras were totally opposed. Siba Singha further continued the policy of persecuting the Sattaras, and soon the conflict was heading to a full fledged revolt.

Sept 15, 1769- Ragh Neog, a well known disciple of the Sattara, was publicly flogged by Ahom officials for not supplying the requisite elephants. Neog rebelleed in November against the Ahom rulers, with the support of Mohanmala, the brother of Lakshmi Singha, and overthrew him. Neog took over the throne, and executed many high ranking Ahom officials. However his inexperience in ruling showed up, and he turned out to be as corrupt as the leaders whom he overthrow. He forcibly took the wives and daughters of many nobles into his harem, and the rebels were left dissatisfied. Taking advantage, some of the nobles, killed Ragh in 1770, and a major purge was carried out on his associates and followers.

The rebels regrouped again under Govinda Gaoburha, and attempted to overthrow the king. With a slogan of*praja-oi joroiroa, chekani-oi sopai dhora*("Ye' oppressed subjects, hold your stave close"), this revolt was called*chekani kubua ron*("The war of the staves"). While they managed to overthrow the existing Borpatrogohain and the Dhekial Phukan , they were defeated at Thowra, by the new Borpatrogohain, and a cavalry unit of the Manipur king. Govinda was captured and killed, putting down the revolt.

The rebels still continued to indulge in guerilla warfare from the thick jungles of Assam, but soon the Burhagohain struck back ruthlessly. Systematically attacking villages sympathetic to the rebels, blocking food and water to them, the Ahoms managed to capture many of the rebel leaders and suppress the revolt brutally.

The conflict got even worse in April 1783, when the Moran tribe were massacred en masse in reprisal for the rebel attacks in Rangpur and Garhgaon. The rebels struck back more vigorously, and in January, 1788, the Ahom ruler Gaurinatha Singha, had to flee the capital. The rebel leader Harihar Tanti looked after the Northern bank of the Brahmaputra, while another one Howha governed the riverine island of Majuli. However with the aid of the East India company, Gaurinath Singha, recaptured Gauhati by 1792. Though the rebels, tried to recapture the throne, the Ahom rulers this time with a more professional army, managed to put it down ruthlessly.

It was a pyrhhic victory though, as the continous conflict, took a toll on the Ahom kingdom, and weakened, it from within. The Ahom- Vaishnava Sattara conflict, exposed the internal rifts within the Ahom kingdom. The crumbling of the Paik system, ruined the economy, which was totally based on it, and the internal rifts were completely out in the open now.

The subsequent invasions between the Burmese between 1817 and 1826 further weakened the kingdom. And soon the British victory in the First Anglo-Burmese war, meant Assam was ceded to them, effectively bringing to an end the Ahom kingdom. While the Burmese invasions, and British rule, could be taken as factors in the fall of the Ahom kingdom, the Moamoria rebelllion and the collapse of the Paik system, was what began the decline.
